narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.40—above the typical 3.21 average—your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on past students' data to predict bar exam success on the first try. The model assessed your profile, including your Year 1 school decile of 5 (slightly below the average 5.71), Year 3 decile of 4 (below the average 5.53), middle family income bracket, LSAT score of 28.50 (well below the average 36.70), and full-time student status, which is common among applicants. It calculated a 52.7% probability that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt, tipping the scales just over the threshold that led to your application's rejection. This prediction means the school sees a moderate risk that you might not pass initially, based on patterns from similar students, prompting them to deny admission to prioritize those with stronger projected outcomes. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your LSAT score, as its lower-than-average level strongly signaled potential challenges in bar exam preparation compared to peers who succeeded. Counterbalancing that, your undergraduate GPA had the second-strongest positive influence, pulling the prediction toward passing because it exceeds the norm and reflects strong academic capability. Your Year 1 school decile ranking provided a milder positive push as well, being reasonably competitive and helping offset some concerns. Overall, while your GPA and early grades compare favorably to typical students, the LSAT's dominant negative impact outweighed them, aligning your profile more closely with past applicants who struggled on the bar. Many admitted students have LSAT scores closer to or above 36.70, which helps explain why yours stood out as the key hurdle. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score toward the average, as even a modest improvement could shift your predicted odds significantly. Strengthening your application with additional recommendations or relevant experience might also help in reapplying. You're clearly capable based on your GPA, so targeted preparation could turn this around for future opportunities. Keep pushing—many students in similar positions have succeeded by addressing their standout weaknesses head-on.
